I'm thinking -- what if we completely remove the concept of "projects" and everything is just nested channels? thoughts?

Genuinely a good instinct — and worth saying first: the code is already ~80% living in this world. Per your own nouns, a project *is* a top-level NIP-29 group and a channel is the same group with a parent set. "Project" today is just the name for "channel whose parent is empty." So the honest question isn't "can we collapse them" — they're already collapsed — it's **"is there anything `project` carries that doesn't reduce to tree position?"**

I think there's exactly one thing, and it's the crux.

## The one thing that doesn't collapse: the workspace binding

A channel is a pure social/coordination construct. It has members, messages, a name, a parent. It has no *body*.

A project has a body: a git checkout, a working directory, on a specific machine. That's what `@developer5 backend="laptop"` is anchored to. When an agent "joins a project," it isn't just joining a chat — it's being pointed at a repo on disk. Nested channels don't have that; `#reactivity` isn't a second checkout.

So the clean framing isn't *"remove projects, everything is channels."* It's:

> **A project is a channel that owns a workspace.** "Project" stops being a noun and becomes a *role* (or an attribute) a channel can have — the presence of a workspace binding, not a position in the tree.

That reframing is stronger than pure "it's all channels" because it survives the obvious counterexample (a monorepo with two apps = one root channel, two child channels each owning a subtree → child channels that *also* have workspaces → recursive projects, for free).

## What you actually gain

- **One recursion, one membership model.** Roster = channel membership at every level. No "project agents" vs "channel members" duality.
- **Sub-projects / monorepo split fall out naturally** instead of being a special case.
- **The fabric snapshot becomes one tree renderer** instead of project→channels→subchannels being three tiers with different rules.

## The three things that decide whether it's actually clean

1. **Roster inheritance.** Today "the project's agents" is a flat set. In a pure tree, does a subchannel inherit the parent's members, or is membership per-node? This is the real design fork, not the naming. My lean: **membership is per-node, awareness inherits downward** — you're a member where you were added, but you can *see* activity in descendants of channels you're in. Otherwise every subchannel needs re-invites and the ergonomics die.

2. **Where the root anchor lives.** Something still has to answer "which machine, which checkout." If that's an attribute on the root channel, fine — but then "channel" quietly has two shapes (with-workspace / without), which is the `project` distinction wearing a different hat. That's OK as long as it's *one optional field*, not a subtype.

3. **NIP-29 cost.** Every channel is a group. "Everything is nested channels" at scale = a lot of groups on the relay. If sub-work spawns cheap ephemeral channels, you want a story for when a group is worth minting vs. when it's just a thread. (You've already been circling per-session-rooms-optional for exactly this reason.)

## My recommendation

Do it — but rename the goal. Not "kill projects," but **"projects and channels are one recursive node; `project` is the role a node plays when it owns a workspace."** Then the two questions that decide the design are (1) roster inheritance semantics and (2) whether the workspace binding is one optional field on a node or a genuine subtype.

Want me to pressure-test the roster-inheritance fork specifically? That's where I think this either becomes elegant or turns into a re-invite nightmare — everything else is comparatively mechanical.

Here's the design I'd commit to — four calls, in order of how load-bearing they are:

## 1. One node type: `channel`. "Project" becomes an attribute, not a concept.

A channel optionally carries a **workspace binding** (`machine + path`, i.e. what today makes something a project). One optional field on the node — explicitly *not* a subtype, not a different table, not a different code path. The word "project" survives only in human-facing rendering: a workspace-bearing channel gets shown as a project root. Everything in the schema, resolver, and daemon speaks only `channel`.

This matches what your schema already wants — you have one resolver (`channel_resolve.rs`), identity is `(parent, name)`, and the noun files already say "a channel and a project are the same thing." You're not redesigning; you're deleting the last place the duality leaks.

## 2. Membership is per-node; **awareness inherits downward**.

The fork I flagged — here's my call:

- You are a *member* only of channels you were explicitly added to.
- You have *awareness* (fabric snapshot visibility, deltas) of all descendants of channels you're a member of.
- @-mentioning someone from a subchannel they're not in works and reads as what it is: a cross-channel mention, using your existing `@slug@backend` addressing. Replying/joining is the explicit `invite`/`switch` you already locked in decision (C) — no membership side-effects from mentions.

Why this way: inheritance-of-membership makes every subchannel roster huge and meaningless (14 members in `#tenex-edge` → 14 ghosts in every task room). Per-node membership with inherited *visibility* keeps rosters truthful while killing the re-invite tax — you can see the subchannel exists and who's there before deciding to enter. This also composes cleanly with the who/awareness format you've already shipped (subtree presence deltas already do most of this).

## 3. Workspace binding resolves by **nearest ancestor**.

An agent in `tenex-edge/reactivity/perf-hunt` asks "where's my repo?" → walk up to the nearest workspace-bearing ancestor. This one rule is what makes monorepo sub-projects free later (a child channel binding a subdirectory just shadows the root binding) — but you don't have to build multi-binding now. Ship with "root binds, descendants inherit," and the recursion is already correct.

## 4. Don't change the relay contract at all.

Every channel is still a NIP-29 group with a parent hint — that's already true. The migration is purely local: wherever daemon/CLI/hook code branches on "is this a project or a channel," replace with "does this node have a workspace binding" or "is parent empty," whichever the call site actually means. My bet is most call sites mean one of those two and the word "project" was papering over which.

## What I'd explicitly *not* do

- **No subtype/enum** (`kind: project | channel | session`) — that's the old model with a new name, and it'll grow cases.
- **No deep nesting encouragement.** The model supports arbitrary depth; the culture shouldn't. Two levels (root + task rooms) covers ~all real use, and your per-session-rooms-optional decision already points that way. Depth is a capability you get for free, not a feature to promote.
- **No big-bang rename.** `relay_*` tables are relay-sourced projections — leave them. This is a local-state and rendering refactor.

**Sequencing:** file it as one GitHub issue with call #2 (membership vs. awareness) stated as the locked decision, since it's the only genuinely irreversible semantic. #1, #3, #4 are mechanical once #2 is fixed. Want me to draft that issue?
publish a gh issue about this
